perturbation.original_text: 8.4.1. Payment by NICE is due current thirty (30) Days from the date of receipt of the invoice, on the 2nd or 16th of the month, whichever is following the invoice date and may be made by check or wire transfer. Purchase Orders, invoices and payments will be presented and effected in US dollars.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.4.1. Payment by NICE is due current sixty (60) Days from the date of receipt of the invoice, on the 2nd or 16th of the month, whichever is following the invoice date and may be made by check or wire transfer. Purchase Orders, invoices and payments will be presented and effected in US dollars.
perturbation.explanation: Changing payment terms from 30 days to 60 days could be problematic. Many jurisdictions have prompt payment laws. Although this contract is governed by the laws of the State of Israel, it is between an Israeli company and another Israeli company. In the absence of specific details in the contract, we will assume that the agreement is for the provision of manufacturing outsourcing services and are not for the provision of construction services. Therefore, we will default to U.S. Law.
perturbation.law_citation: 31 U.S. Code § 3903 - Interest penalties for failure to make prompt payments
perturbation.law_explanation: The Prompt Payment Act requires federal agencies to pay contractors on time. Changing the payment terms to 60 days delays payments beyond standard time frames and could incur interest penalties.

Regulations (a) The Director of the Office of Management and Budget shall prescribe regulations to carry out section 3902 of this title . The regulations shall- (1) provide that the required payment date is- (A) the date payment is due under the contract for the item of property or service provided; or (B) except as provided in paragraphs (10) and (11), 30 days after a proper invoice for the amount due is received if a specific payment dat
perturbation.justification: The change from 30 to 60 days for payment due date contradicts the Prompt Payment Act, which aims to ensure timely payments to contractors. Scraped Law Snippet 1, citing 31 USC 3903, mentions regulations related to prompt payment under section 3902, specifying that the required payment date should be either the date agreed in the contract or 30 days after receiving a proper invoice, unless exceptions apply. By extending the payment term to 60 days, the modified contract violates the standard timeframe established by the Prompt Payment Act, creating an inconsistency with federal regulations.

perturbation.original_text: 15.1. Except as provided in Sections 15.2 and 15.3, any breach by any Party of this Agreement which was not remedied within forty-five (45) Days from the date of notice, shall be regarded as a fundamental breach.
perturbation.changed_text: 15.1. Except as provided in Sections 15.2 and 15.3, any breach by any Party of this Agreement which was not remedied within ten (10) Days from the date of notice, shall be regarded as a fundamental breach.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period for a breach from 45 days to 10 days creates an inconsistency. While the laws of the State of Israel govern this contract, we will also use US law as a reference. The change drastically shortens the time to remedy a breach, and may be a contradiction of law.
perturbation.law_citation: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-302 - Unconscionable Contract or Clause
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification could be deemed unconscionable or against good faith principles because it provides an unreasonably short time frame to cure a breach. UCC § 2-302 allows a court to refuse to enforce a contract or clause found to be unconscionable at the time it was made. This would depend on the specifics of the breach, but the clause now has the potential to be unconscionable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. (1) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (2) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose and effect to aid the court in making the determination. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The change from 45 days to 10 days to remedy a breach directly contradicts principles of good faith and conscionability as potentially violating UCC § 2-302, detailed in Scraped Law Snippet 1. The modified contract provides an unreasonably short time frame to cure a breach, giving the impression of being unconscionable at the time it was made. The modified text weakens a legal requirement because a court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result, which is a clear violation of the law.
